coredata.dc:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Salvia hispanica L. seeds and investigation of their biodistribution, and cytotoxicity effects
coredata.dc:creator: Marouzi, Somayeh
Darroudi, Majid
Hekmat, Azadeh
Sadri, Kayvan
Kazemi Oskuee, Reza
coredata.dc:description: The unique properties of water-soluble CQDs, as well as their physical and optical features obtained during in the processing of the natural materials, have further motivated researchers towards their usage for increasing human safety and reducing environmental pollution. The aim of this study was to investigate the formation of nutrient Salvia hispanica L . (Chia) as a new potential carbon source through a hydrothermal strategy, as well as to analyze the biological distribution and cytotoxicity of CQDs. The obtained CQDs were evaluated based on various physicochemical procedures and according to the results, they were observed to contain an average size of 5.4 nm, a spherical morphology, a satisfactory fluorescence, and quantum yield (QY) of 17.8% in optimal conditions (180 °C for 4 h). In addition, the non-toxicity and colloidal stability of CQDs in the was confirmed through the performed studies under different physiological conditions. We also tested their biological distribution by applying CQDs-99mTc labeling in the appointed rats. Overall, this report demonstrates the cost-effectiveness, biocompatibility, and bioavailability of CQDs, which can be considered as a novel potential source for centralized medical imaging.

head.ce:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Salvia hispanica L. seeds and investigation of their biodistribution, and cytotoxicity effects

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unique properties of water-soluble CQDs, as well as their physical and optical features obtained during in the processing of the natural materials, have further motivated researchers towards their usage for increasing human safety and reducing environmental pollution. The aim of this study was to investigate the formation of nutrient Salvia hispanica L . (Chia) as a new potential carbon source through a hydrothermal strategy, as well as to analyze the biological distribution and cytotoxicity of CQDs. The obtained CQDs were evaluated based on various physicochemical procedures and according to the results, they were observed to contain an average size of 5.4 nm, a spherical morphology, a satisfactory fluorescence, and quantum yield (QY) of 17.8% in optimal conditions (180 °C for 4 h). In addition, the non-toxicity and colloidal stability of CQDs in the was confirmed through the performed studies under different physiological conditions. We also tested their biological distribution by applying CQDs-99mTc labeling in the appointed rats. Overall, this report demonstrates the cost-effectiveness, biocompatibility, and bioavailability of CQDs, which can be considered as a novel potential source for centralized medical imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, the number of performed researches on the topic of nanomaterials, especially carbon quantum dots (CQDs), has increased due to discovering the existence of unlimited carbon resources in nature. In this regard, CQDs are accounted as potential competitors in conventional semiconductor quantum dots due to their impressive and innovative features [1,2] that include easy and cheap synthesis, chemical immobility, adjustable light radiation, and excellent biocompatibility [3,4]. In addition, their potential features make them applicable for certain for studies such as chemical sensing [5–9], biosensing [10–14], biodistribution [15,16], photocatalysis [16–19], bioimaging [20–24], and fluorescence labelling [25–28]. Moreover, CQD-based analysis techniques are cost-effective, highly sensitive, and easy to apply [29]. Nowadays, green chemistry has created a new and productive approach for the synthesis of nanoparticles, as well as the processing and usage of chemicals that can increase human health and environmental compatibility [30–32]. Nanoparticles are produced through a variety of renewable natural carbon sources such as plant extracts, fungi, algae, yeasts, and bacteria [33–35]. Plant extracts are obtained from various parts of plants, including leaves, bark, stems, seeds, flowers, and roots, that can function as preservatives/stabilizers throughout the synthesis of nanoparticles [36–38]. Recently, the development of CQDs have been performed by conventional methods such as hydrothermal [31,39,40], microwave assisted [41–43], electrochemical oxidation [44], chemical oxidation [45,46], ultrasonic methods, and etc [47,48]. However, the hydrothermal synthesis of these materials, involving the usage of various sources of renewable and natural carbon, has significantly progressed in comparison to other available physical methods (laser ablation, arc discharge, and plasma treatment) that are eco-friendly, green, and cheap [49]. Moreover, considering its simplicity and quantum performance, this procedure has proved to be suitable for CQDs production, which had been carried out in previous studies through the application of natural renewable sources in the role of precursors such as apple juice [45], coffee grounds [50], orange juice & peel [51,52], watermelon peel [53], onion juice [54], pomelo peel [55], chitosan [56], ginger leaves [57], chicken blood [58], gelatin [59], and low-cost organic wastes [60–62]. In this regard, the cultivation of chia (Salvia hispanica L.) faced a worldwide increase in recent years due to its popularity and healthy properties as a dietary supplement. Chia seeds are also used as a potential source of nutrients in the United States, Latin America, and Australia [63]. The main ingredients of this substance is chia seeds, carbohydrates, dietary fiber, proteins, and fatty acids [63,64]. Pictures of seeds and plants of chia are represented in Fig. 1 a and b, respectively.

Next to being remarkable in terms of thickening and gelation, synergy control, emulsion stabilization, and etc [65,66], this material is widely applied in various applications of food industry due to its hydrocolloid and water retention abilities. Furthermore, the accommodation of certain elements, such as carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, has qualified the utilization of this substance as a carbon-rich source by the synthesis of CQDs through top-down methods [67]. According to the available reports, performing a hydrothermal procedure on cellulose paper that contain MoS2 can provide CQDs that are derived from a one-step pyrolytic process of chia seeds; this product can be exerted for the fabrication of low-cost flexible broadband optical devices [68]. The synthesized CQDs obtained by pyrolysis and the usage of chia seeds, along with the involvement of diffusion fluorophores, were reported as a probe for biological imaging. Being in conformity with the gathered results, carbon dots can interact with specific solvents such as water, acetone, and N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) as it can be observed throughout its properties e.g., photoluminescence spectra (PL) and colloidal stability [67]. As it is known, compounds that contain radioactive properties are also capable of emitting radiation, since as a result of the decay effect, the initial unstable nucleus becomes a more stable nucleus or smaller nuclei [69]. The 99mTc isotope, which contain a half-life of 6 h, displayed the shortest lifetime in emitting gamma rays (γ) in order to turn into its stable isotope. The utilization of radionuclides serves two purposes including (i) the diagnostic goals and (ii) therapeutic goals [70], while their application can be determined through the type of emitted radiation. In diagnostic radiotherapy, a gamma irradiation (γ) is required for detecting the radiation that is emitted outside of the body, however in therapeutic radiotherapy, the applied radionuclide must produce particle radiation to attack and destroy the targeted tissue [71,72]. The utilization of carbon dots could be considered for performing diagnostic and targeted imaging due to their physicochemical properties e.g., size, morphology, and surface potentials. In recent decades, the ability of labeling carbon nanotubes with specific radio-activities such as 99mTc, 123I, and 177Lu stands as an alternative approach for the available options in cancer detection and treatment [73]. Therefore, this study was developed with the aim of highlighting a simple, economical, and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method for the preparation of fluorescent CQDs, which is processed through the application of Chia seeds as a new carbon source. The prepared CQDs were observed to be highly water-soluble and nano-sized (5.4 ± 1.0 nm), while emitting bright green fluorescence. We also characterized their surface chemistry, size, and morphology by the employment of FT-IR, Zeta potential, XRD, photoluminescence, UV–Vis spectrophotometry, and TEM. The in vivo imaging studies that involved the usage of 99mTc radioisotope were performed by labeling the obtained CQDs, while in addition, their cell toxicity was tested on kidney cells line in order to take advantage of their potential in clinical diagnosis.

The CQDs of this work were produced by the usage of chia (Salvia hispanica L .) seeds that were procured from Latin America. Double-distilled water (DDW) was applied throughout the preparation process and for the purpose of evaluating their cytotoxicity, HEK293 (human embryonic kidney) cells line was cultured within a Dulbecco's Medium that was modified with 10% FBS, streptomycin (100 mg/ml), penicillin (100 mg/ml), and MTT (Sigma, 98%). In regards to the labeling experiments, the 99mTc, achieved in saline as Na99mTcO4, was obtained from 99mMo/99mTc generator (Kimia Pakhsh). Hydrochloric acid (HCl), stannous chloride (SnCl2•2H2O) as the reducing agent, acetone (CH3COCH3, 99%), methanol (CH3OH, 98%), and all the other involved materials were purchased from Merck, Germany. Animal studies included Ketamine (10%) and Xylazin (2%) from Merck. Deionized water (DW) was also used throughout the performed experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from seeds of Chia
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare CQDs, 1.0 g of chia seeds was heated in 100 ml of DW to reach the temperature of 60 °C and then, the obtained mucilage was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 4 h. Once the resultant brownish solution gradually reached the room temperature, it was dispersed by sonication for 30 min, which was afterwards centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to eliminate the larger sized particles. As the next step, the obtained brown transparent solution was filtered by the usage of a syringe filter (Pore size: 0.22 µm) to remove the larger carbon nanoparticles. Finally, the produced CQDs were stored at 4 ℃ for further applications, which included zeta potential and atomic force microscopy (AFM), as well as being lyophilized for X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and toxicity analyses. The described procedure is schematically illustrated in Fig. 2.

The functional groups of CQDs were characterized through the application of FTIR (Avatar 370, Thermo Nicolet, USA) within the infrared range of 4000–400 cm-1 and a resolution of 4.0 cm-1, while using the compressed KBr pellets S/N: 15,000 with the scan number of 4 and DTGS detector.

As it is known, crystallographic analysis is based on the X-ray wavelength characteristics of the utilized device (XRD, X'PertPro Cu Kα; λ = 0.154 nm, Panalytical Co, Holland). The nuclei of atoms are located in a crystalline grid positioned at a short distance (about a few angstroms) from each other. Although X-ray crystallography (XRD) was observed to collide with crystals in which the atoms or the order are located, however, they apparently scatter over amorphous materials [74]. The wide XRD peak at 18.3° is in correspondence to the (002) Bragg's reflection.

The CQDs samples were identified through the usage of UV–Vis spectrophotometry and recorded at room temperature. The gathered measurement information includes the cell length (10 mm), data pitch (1.0 nm), and band width (1.5 nm) in a scanning speed of 400 nm/min.

Certain types of nanomaterials and some molecular species are categorized as luminescent compounds. CQDs proved to contain fluorescence properties when being sized in nanoscale, which can be determined through the fluorescence spectrometer of their absorption and emission wavelengths. In this regard, their Photoluminescent (PL) spectra were recorded by the employment of an F-2500 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) that was equipped with 1.0 cm quartz. Cells, as well as a bath thermostat with Xe-light and 0.1 nm fluorescence life-time. We also settled the scan rate on 1200 nm/min and utilized a photoelectric multiplier tube (PMT) voltage of 700 V, while the excitation wavelength (λex) was set at 290 nm in an aqueous solution. In prior to perform the data analysis, emission intensities were corrected for inner filter and dilution effects.
Zeta potential (ζ) measurements were carried out by the usage of a SZ-100z Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) & Zeta potential analyzer, Horiba Co, Japan.

We evaluated the crystal structure details of synthetic CQDs by the means of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis via Zeiss (Leo 912 AB, Germany) at 100 kV with a magnification of 80–500,000 times. The results were obtained after preparing an ultrasonic synthesized sample for 5 min, which was dried on a copper-carbon film at room temperature. As the last step, the image was analyzed by the application of ImageJ® software.

The topography of synthesized CQDs surfaces was reported through the usage of atomic force scanning microscope (AFM), Brisk model (Research Ara Co., Iran) of Iran, which contained an XY-scanner, 50 µm maximum scan range, and 1.0 nm resolution, as well as a Z-scanner 4 µm maximum scan range with a resolution of 0.1 nm. The procedure was completed by preparing an ultrasonic sample for 5 min and having it diluted on a glass slide at room temperature.

We performed the cultivation of HEK293 cell line within a clean atmosphere, for which the cells were cultured in a DMEM that contained 10% fetal bovine and 1.0% penicillin-streptomycin. In the following, the cells were cultured and propagated under standard conditions in accordance with the protocol of 37 °C, humid conditions, and 5% CO2.

An in vitro method, known as bromide 3-(4–4-Dimethyldiazylazol-2-yl)-2,5-imethylphenazole (MTT), was utilized to examine the cytotoxicity of synthesized CQDs and determine the percentage of cells survival in the kidney line cells in comparison to the control group. Briefly, the toxicity effect of CQDs on HEK293 cell lines was implanted and incubated in a 96-well plate with a density of 5 × 103 cells/well at 37 °C under the CO2 atmosphere (5.0%) for 24 h. Thereafter, 100 μl of CQDs was added to the DMEM in the final concentrations of 0–500 µg/ml through performing three repetitions, while the empty wells containing suspension were considered as blanks. In the following, we incubated the plates for 48 h at 37 °C in the incubator and subsequent to 48 h, had the environment of all the plates changed under the hood. in each well, 150 μl of medium with the FBS of 2.0% and 500 μl of MTT solution with 5 mg/ml concentration was poured in order to incubate the plates for 4 h in an incubator. The next step required to remove the contents of all the wells and add 200 μl of DMSO to each well for the purpose of eliminating the MTT crystals that were revived by the cells. At this point, they were immediately read at 570 nm (Biotek Instrument, Winooski, VT, USA) and compared with the control group by the usage of a microplate absorber (optical density). We calculated the percentage of living cells and the amount of toxicity as the relative percentage of mean ± SD.

Where, Aexp stands as the absorbance value of the test and Actrl represents the amount of blank adsorbed (DMEM without cells), while a positive would be the absorbance value of positive control (DMEM cells).

Flow cytometry analysis can provide the opportunity for accurately measuring the fluorescent signals to determine the survival rate of large populations of synthesized HEK293 cells treated with CQDs while facilitating control samples that are not treated with CQDs as well. For this purpose, we incubated the HEK293 cells in 12 wells that contained a density of 2 × 104 cells/well to be implanted under the standard conditions for 24 h. Then, subsequent to adding the right amounts (62.5, 125, and 250 µg/ml) of CQDs to the HEK293 cells line, they were re-incubated for 48 h. The wells environment was changed and the cells were washed twice with phosphate buffer salt (PBS). Once reaching the trypsinization phase, the dead and dying cells were removed to undergo subsequent washes with 1.0 ml of PBS and the usage of centrifugation at 1500 rpm in room temperature for 5 min. Based on the protocols of apoptosis measurement kit, the resulting cells were subjected to 500 μl of a solution containing Annexin V-FITC/ Propidium iodide (PI) dye, which were then incubated for 15 min. As the last step, the apoptosis value of stained cells were measured by the employment of a FACSVerse flow flowmeter (Becton, Dickinson and Company, Franklin Lakes, NJ, USA).

One of the applied studies in this paper was the labeling of nanoparticles in regards to diagnostic and therapeutic aspects and for this purpose, we labeled the synthesized CQDs from chia seeds by the application of technetium that were obtained from the molybdenum generator (99mMo/99mTc); hence, the CQDs labeling process began with the formation of tin-chloride aqueous solution (2.0 mg of SnCl2 in 2.0 ml of HCl 0.1 M). Thereafter, 100 μl of SnCl2 solution as the reducing agents [75] and 500 μl of 99mTcO4 (3 mCi) were added to 1.0 ml of synthesized CQDs. In order to perform a better reaction, the stock was incubated at 1400 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the efficacy and stability of CQDs labeled 99mTcO4 were assessed by the means of Instant Thin Layer Chromatography (ITLC) [76].

The labeling efficiency (%) of CQDs was evaluated through the ITLC. In addition, the colloidal stability of CQDs-99mTc in DW and serum was examined in two solvent reservoirs of chromatography 85% methanol and acetone as a mobile phase at the time intervals beginning from 0 up to 24 h by ITLC [77]. Furthermore, the amount of radioactivity was determined by the three pieces that were obtained from the dried ITLC sheets in standard RIA tubes located in the Gama counter (Well Gamma Delshid, DL 100).

The study of CQDs-99mTc cell binding was performed on HEK293 cells line by placing 1 × 106 cells/well under standard conditions for 72 h. Thereafter, we removed the supernatant and washed the cells three times with PBS to have them positioned in anti-gamma tubes. Lastly, the activity of cells and supernatant was required to be calculated by gamma counter along with measuring the removal or binding percentage of CQDs-99mTc to the target cells.

Considered as the animal model, a number of adult male rats of the Wistar breed (weighing 200–250 g) was exerted in controlled conditions to analyze the in vivo distribution of CQDs-99mTc, which included the temperature of 24 °C and a 12 h light-dark cycle that began from natural light. The rats were divided into three groups of 3 members and injected with 200 μl of CQDs-99mTc through the lateral tail vein in the time intervals of 1, 4, and 24. Subsequently, the rats that displayed CQDs-99mTc activity were sacrificed at the labeled times to have their blood taken and weight the other organs including stomach, lungs, intestines, liver, spleen, kidneys, brain, tail and thyroid, etc. The accumulated dose rate of each tissue was expressed as the percentage of injected dose (%ID)/g of organ [78], while the amount of activity per organ was calculated by placing the isolated organs inside the gamma counter [79].

This study is based upon national animal imaging laws and therefore, one of the nine rats was rapidly anesthetized with xylene and ketamine. In the following, CQDs-99mTc were biologically distributed throughout the body and monitored through a high-resolution gamma camera (Siemens Double, Germany) with low energy consumption (20%). The static and anterior posterior images were obtained after 6 and 24 h of injection, which required the usage of a 256 × 256 matrix for a total of 300,000 counts with a zoom factor of 2 at 10 min

The structural characteristics of synthesized CQDs and Chia seeds extract were analyzed by the application of FTIR spectra. Fig. 3 represents the FTIR analysis that was performed to determine the characteristics of CQDs and chia seed extract. In comparison to the case of chia seed extract, the FTIR spectrum of synthesized CQDs displayed some typical peaks. The location of the main peak, which occurred at 3373 cm-1, can be attributed to the stretching vibration of O‒H, N‒H group while being indicative of its water absorption and confirming the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of sample. In fact, it can be assumed that as the particle size of an object decreases, the number of its surface elements significantly increases. Due to the fact that the surface of CQDs is capable of absorbing moisture, a wide absorption band can be always observed at 3200–3500 cm-1 through the FTIR spectrum [80]. Moreover, the conversion of carbon source (chia seeds) or the hydrocarbon to a graphical structure was indicated by the blue shift of the peak and the reduction of peaks with C‒H stretching vibration index at 2931 cm-1 compared to the peak similar to that of the chia seeds mucilage, as well as the presence of three weak peaks at 1773, 1704 and 1430 cm-1, the small amounts of C˭O, N‒H and C‒N, respectively, which are detectable at the CQD level [81–83]. However, the appearance of peaks within the range of 1596 and 1282 cm-1 can be attributed to the vibrational attractiveness of C˭C and C‒O alkoxy groups, respectively [84,85]. Also, the detected peak at 1415 cm-1 was related to the bending vibration of O‒H groups [86,87], whereas the peaks at 1075, 780, 669 and 623 cm-1 were attributed to C‒O stretches and out-of-plane C‒H bending vibrations [88]. The high solubility of CQD in water was reported by the results, which was due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups ‒COOH, ‒OH, and ‒NH2. Therefore, it can be stated by considering the outcomes of FTIR and the zeta potential procedures that the obtained CQDs level has an inherent negative charge that leads to its uniform dispersion in aqueous solutions for a long time and in this respect, they can be considered for biological imaging [81,89].

The XRD result displayed in ( Fig. 4), which illustrated a single broad peak at about 18.3 degree, confirmed the amorphous structure of CQDs based on JCPDS # 41-1487 (Graphite) [90,91]. The distance interlayer of 4.84 in the 18.3 degree confirmed the existence of a graphitic structure [92]. In addition, the XRD analyze provided data on the purity of C-dots that was caused by the complete chia-carbonization [93,94]. Besides, the CQD solution demonstrated excellent stability over a long time of storage (Fig. 1, inset) due to the ‒C˭H and ‒OH bonding, and π-π* transition (FTIR analyze) [95]. According to Rietveld refinement analysis, the XRD diffraction data exhibited a perfect matching with the Cif-file reference of 9014004 (Carbon) [96], which is associated with the semi-crystalline structure of as-synthesized CQDs. Rietveld refinement method revealed the particle size of every C-dot, comparable with size-extracted via TEM analyze at 5.4 nm, while the DLS method was only abled to measure the bulk-aggregated form of CQDs.

Fig. 5 displays the obtained UV–Vis spectrometry that was used to validate the quality of CQDs [97]. According to the performed electronic excitation, the maximum absorption of CQDs that were synthesized through ultraviolet light was observed at 280 nm due to the transferring of π-π∗ and presence of C˭C (aromatic carbon) functional groups, as well as the occurrence of n- π∗ transfers that were associated with the C˭O (carbonyl) bonds caused in the form of a tiny shoulder between the range of 350–370 nm [43], which indicates the formation of CQDs. The synthesized CQDs exhibited an strong optical absorption throughout the ultraviolet (200–400 nm) area while its tail was extended towards the visible range (600 nm). Due to the type of exerted synthesizing method, the obtained size and chemistry of quantum nanoparticles surface resulted in a unique feature known as fluorescence and therefore, it can be assumed that the intensity and propagation of fluorescent rays is dependent on the wavelength of excitation [98]. In this work, the CQDs were reported to be yellow amber in daylight and bright green when being exposed to 365 nm of ultraviolet light. The figure inset displayed a surface chemistry of water-soluble CQDs with green luminescence.

From a fundamental and practical point of view, among the effects of quantum constraints, the fluorescence property can be considered as the most attractive feature of CQDs [69]. CQDs were derived from various and natural carbon sources in many studies and reports, however their stimulation through ultraviolet light resulted in emitting luminescence spectrum at different wavelengths [75,99]. The emission of certain colors including light-green, yellow, or blue can be detected by considering the appropriateness of excitation wavelength, which would be in accordance with the size and chemical performance of applied CQDs [76]. Therefore, the optical properties of this material can be altered by adjusting their size, shape, and geometry, as well as the nature of their edges and band gaps [77]. The presented PL spectrum in Fig. 6 is related to the CQDs that were derived from a simple one-pot hydrothermal method without involving any substance other than the required chia seeds. In this regard, we prepared a solution of CQDs (2000 μg/ml) by the usage of an ultrasonic bath for capturing the fluorescence spectrum, λem, and λex. Upon being exposed to the stimulus wavelength of 310 nm, the emission of CQDs at 415 nm was perceived with the maximum scattering intensity of fluorescence. Furthermore, the two absorption peaks observed at 280 and 310 nm can be attributed to the center of carbon nucleus and to the surface or center of molecule, respectively [75,99]. In general, this behavior can be referred to the presence of at least two types of excitation energy traps on the surface of CQDs. In addition, the dependency of green CQDs in regards to stimulus wavelengths with an increase of 20 nm, from 290 to 470 nm, was indicative of a red-shift from 415 to 527 nm. The displacement of λem. towards a larger number of wavelengths can suggest the dependency of λem. on λex. Also, the optimization of synthesized CQDs in particle size and amount of functional groups that are present on their surface can result in optimizing the λex, which can have a significant effect on λem [100,101]. Moreover, the high energy content of nanoparticles such as CQDs can increase their tendency towards accumulation, which not only decreases the emission intensity of fluorescence but also results in longer wavelengths and reduced band gaps or electron layers. According to the observations and performed studies on the spectrum behaviors of CQDs, the irritability of CQDs can be displayed by using visible light throughout the wavelength regions of > 600 nm, while the luminescence radiation areas are apparently located at wavelengths < 600 nm [102]. The dependency of luminescence mechanism on λex. is ambiguous and consequently, scientists blame the deficiency level of CQDs as a phenomenon of luminescence and consider it as a stimulant of energy for creating luminescence in certain fields such as imaging, diagnosis, and treatment [103]. In regards to the efficient measurement of green photon emission (quantum yield (QY)), the fluorescent of CQDs has been determined according to a comparative method that involve the usage of quinine sulfate as the standard reference [104]. The fluorescent QYCQDs was found to be about 17.8%. Eq. (2) was exerted to calculate the fluorescence QY obtained through the experimental data of synthesized CQDs sample and the application of quinine sulphate (QY is 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the standard sample [105,106].

In general, QYs represents the quantum efficiency of fluorescence for a standard reference. Under constant λex, for instance 360 nm, ICQDs and Is display the uniform diffusion intensity of synthesized CQDs and standard samples, respectively. The absorbance symptoms are presented by ACQDs and as of the prepared sample and standard sample at the similar in λex, respectively. Lastly, η represents the refractive index of the same solvents that were previously utilized.

Zeta Potential (ζ-potential) analysis is a method for determining the electric charge of particles. The presence of surface load on CQDs stands as the main reason behind the stability of colloids, which also causes the nanoparticles to repel each other and fail in connecting and forming larger clumps (69). Therefore, it can be assumed that the stability of colloids can be increased by heightening the surface charge of colloidal CQDs. Fig. 7 a and b represent the performed ζ-potential analysis of synthesized CQDs and 99mTc-labeled CQDs, respectively. The occurrence of an interaction between CQDs and 99mTc radioisotope caused a slight alteration, from − 16 to − 7.6 mV, throughout the electric charge of prepared CQDs subsequent to being marked with 99mTc. In addition, the stability of CQDs-99mTc was indicated by confirming the binding of 99mTc to the CQDs level. The results of DLS analysis is dependent on the effect of incident laser light on an analyte that contain spherical particles with natural brown motion. Scatter light exists in each direction with different wavelengths and intensities as a function of time. Therefore, the light scattering intensity of the nanoparticles is equal to the sixth power of the particle radius [107]. Increasing the particle size of incident light wavelength (> λ/10) causes Mie’s anisotropic scattering [108,109]. The amount of energy of radiant light is unequal to the amount of energy of incident light (Inelastic scattering), which id dependent on the angle. Thus, the diffusion coefficient measured by the DLS represents the equivalent hydrodynamic size [110]. Furthermore, we represented a summary of the results obtained from the analysis of zeta potential and average hydrodynamic diameters in Table 1 . Similar to the outcomes of other studied methods, this work also suggests that labeling CQDs with 99mTc can stand as a promising approach for performing targeted diagnosis throughout the body.

In the measurement techniques of nanoparticles are slightly different scientific mechanisms and measurement methods. The TEM microscopy is a single particle-based approach referred to as a gold standard technique for measuring the nanoparticles size using electrons instead of photons that is examined only the metal core in the plane of view dimensions [111]. Two-dimensional (2D) images indicate information about the crystal structure, size, and morphology of the nanoparticles [112]. Particle size distribution and morphology of the CQDs obtained from chia seeds are exhibited in the TEM images of Fig. 8 and as it can be observed, they contained a spherical shape with sizes in the range of nanoscale, along with uniformed and somewhat cumulative distribution. Considering the presence of functional groups and the higher ratio of surface area to volumes, the fact of existing tendency of CQDs towards Vander-Waals interaction and their accumulation was assumed. The average size of 5.4 ± 1.9 nm was measured in accordance with the particle size distribution in Fig. 8 b [113].

Despite the scientific and quantitative differences in measuring CQDs between AFM and TEM techniques. The AFM microscopy has been used to further examine the size and shape parameters. The AFM shows the metal core and dehydrated particles coating with the height dimension (Z) [111]. Therefore, the AFM is able to determine the surface topography of materials at the nanoscale vertically by measuring the interaction between the probe tip and the sample. Information obtained from AFM can include three-dimensional (3D) topography, mechanical properties such as: elastic modulus of restraint, etc [114]. Surface morphological studies of synthetic CQDs obtained by the means of AFM are displayed in Fig. 9. Similarly, it was demonstrated by AFM image (Fig. 9 a) of CQDs spin-coated on silicon wafer [115] that the CQDs contained an spherical nature in the monodispersion state of nanoparticles. The height and shape of CQDs that was observed in the AFM image (Fig. 9 b) recommended a vertical dimension of 9.9 nm with a scale of 1 × 1 µm. Also, some reports the effective factor contributing to differences between TEM and AFM results have expressed the surface concentration of dense nanoparticles on top of the mica sheet [116,117]. Both TEM (Fig. 8) and AFM results (the number-based particle measurements) were indicative of similar morphology with sizes in the range of < 10 nm. The small size of CQDs diameter was observed to be in the range of 20–40 nm through the Imager Ara software®, which was consistent with the TEM results [118,119].

Biocompatibility stands as one of the most important characteristics of CQDs in the field of medical applications. In order to study the cytotoxicity of CQDs, we incubated 5 × 103 cells in each well within an incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Thereafter, HEK293 cells were exposed to the varying concentrations of 0, 15.5, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 μg/ml for 48 h. According to Fig. 10, the viability of HEK293 cells displayed negligible toxicity subsequent to being incubated with 3- (4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl bromide) -2-diphenyltetrazolium (MTT) for 48 h. In addition, the survival rate of HEK293 cells was even higher than > 90% up to the concentration of 500 µg/ml. In general, These outcomes can be considered as the resultants of using an organic source, as well as the biocompatible and degradable features of the applied synthesizing method that proves the biocompatibility of synthesized CQDs [120,121].

We examined the macrophage apoptosis and necrosis of HEK293 cell line, after having them exposed to synthesized CQDs at the concentrations of 0, 62.5, 125, and 250 μg/ml, in order to evaluate their environmental compatibility and become aware of their potential impacts on human health. This work includes the application of a flow cytometry device and the modified method of staining annexes V-FITC and PI, while the visible areas of Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4 in Fig. 11 were indicative of necrotic, late apoptosis, early apoptosis, and viability cells, respectively [122,123]. According to the results, there were no signs of apoptosis at Q3 phase based on the control group and all the involved concentrations (62.5, 125, and 250 μg/ml). However, the highest percentage of apoptosis obtained from the Q2 phase at a concentration of 250 μg/ml was 3.25%, which is comparable to the necrotic 5.01% of cells at a similar concentration. The increased dependency of apoptotic cells on the concentration of CQDs was suggestive of their low sensitivity towards the concentration of CQDs. Therefore, we assumed that the synthesized CQDs contain a very low toxicity not only due to the low percentage of apoptosis in comparison to cell necrosis, but also by comparing the percentage of living cells that existed in control group and CQDs-treated HEK293 cells throughout different concentrations over 48 h [124]. In general, it can be stated that we offered a biocompatible and degradable CQDs synthesizing method with respect to the organic source of CQDs, which can confirm the biocompatibility of resultant [16,123].

We performed labeling studies in regards to the synthesized CQDs by the usage of 99mTc radioisotope, which were in terms of radiographic frequency stability on two systems including human serum and double distilled water for 24 h to be considered as a prerequisite for in vivo studies and biodistribution. The tracking efficiencies of CQDs-99mTc were obtained by the means of ITLC throughout the two mentioned systems and reported to be 98% and 93%, respectively, at 0, 1, 4, and 24 h after the labeling process. Furthermore, the two systems of human serum and distilled water were studied in mobile phases including acetone and methanol (85%), as well as ITLC. Subsequent to 24 h, CQDs-99mTc in the distilled water system with the moving phase of chromatography, acetone, and 85% methanol solvent displayed a stability of 92% and 93%, respectively. In addition, we investigated the stability of CQDs-99mTc in the human serum system with respect to 98% and 92% of chromatographic solvents and reached the assumption that the level of stability in human serum was more satisfying. According to the results of ζ-potential and average size of nanoparticles that were labeled and unlabeled with 99mTc radioisotope, the observed negative charge reduction and electrostatic forces from CQDs-99mTc level were indicative of the effect of 99mTc linkage with CQDs.

The CQDs-99mTc cell binding was studied on HEK293 cells at the concentrations of 30 and 90 μg/ml, which were added to each well of the utilized 6-wells cell culture plate to be incubated for 3 h. The remaining CQDs were washed three times by the usage of PBS and covered with fresh PBS. As the next step, the amount of radioactivity in gamma camera tubes was measured. According to the results, the cellular binding percentage of CQDs-99mTc was 50% in low concentration and 18% in high concentration. It can be concluded that the accumulation of CQDs high concentrations caused their internal absorption in the cell due to containing an small size and spherical morphology [73,125].

One of the most challenging issues after treating living organisms with nanoparticles is their accurate localization in diagnosis. In this biological distribution study, we applied CQDs into rats through the usage of radiolabeling and performed the subsequent detection process of emitted gamma (γ) photons [126]. In this regard, the rats were divided into three groups, each containing 3 members, and CQDs-99mTc was injected. Subsequently, we sacrificed the selected rats and had their organs separated and examined for counting the photon single of γ by the application of gamma counter machine after 1, 4, and 24 h of injection. The performed calculations of radioactive uptake in the organs are represented in Table 2. In comparison to the other tissues, the high amount of ٪ ID/g in the kidney tissues of rats in every experiment was indicative of the significant accumulation of CQDs-99mTc. According to the results, the highest amount of CQDs-99mTc in the kidneys after 1 and 4 h was observed to be 38.84 ± 1.789 and 47.74 ± 7.484 ٪ID/g, respectively, whereas subsequent to 24 h, the accumulation of labeled nanoparticles was slightly decreased to 31.82 ± 5.953 %ID/g. However, spleen, thyroid, and muscle tissues displayed the lowest values, respectively. The presence of CQDs-99mTc in the circulatory system and their supply to other organs after 24 h can stand as a testament to their stability and optimal permeability, which was caused by the surface tendencies of labeled nanoparticles towards kidney tissue cells [78,127].

In order to evaluate the in vivo imaging potential of CQDs-99mTc, we randomly selected one of the three rats of every group and kept it under detection for 24 h. The imaging process was done on the selected rat with physical inhibition through the subcutaneous injection of ketamine and xylazine [77]. Thereafter, anterior and posterior static images were recorded for 6 and 24 h by the application of a gamma camera that contained a high resolution and required a low-energy. According to Fig. 12, the radioactivity intensity of CQDs-99mTc was significantly higher throughout the kidneys, while the captured images were in conformity with biological distribution data.

Collectively, the synthesized CQDs by chia seed were introduced as a pure biological resource that was obtained through a biocompatible one-step hydrothermal method. Due to containing an average size of 5.4 nm and displaying light green fluorescence with QY (17.8%), the synthesized CQDs exhibited excellent optical properties and colloidal stability in water. In addition, the results of in vitro studies at different concentrations were indicative of their low toxicity on HEK293 cells line. Furthermore, based on flow cytometry, the survival rate of control cells was 92.8% while the study group contained CQDs with a concentration of 250 μg/ml (91.7%). Therefore, their survival throughout the HEK293 cell line at 250 μg/ml was slightly altered due to the biological distribution of CQDs in kidney tissues. Considering these facts, the lack of cytotoxicity, small size, ideal fluorescent properties, and specific accumulation in kidney tissues nominated CQDs as one of the strongest candidates in the field of medicine and applied sciences in terms of fluorescent tracking, cell imaging, and targeted diagnostic research.
